---
title: "Henkin construction and model existence"
---

::: {#lem-pure-language-extension .lemma name="Fresh symbols preserve consistency" date="2026-07-12"}
If \(\Sigma\) is consistent in \(L\) and \(L'\) is obtained by adjoining any
set of new constants, then \(\Sigma\), regarded as an \(L'\)-theory, is
consistent.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-pure-language-extension"}
If an \(L'\)-derivation made \(\Sigma\) inconsistent, it would be finite by
@def-hilbert-calculus and hence mention only finitely many new constants.
@lem-constant-elimination would translate it to an inconsistent
\(L\)-derivation, contrary to consistency of \(\Sigma\) in
@def-consistency.
:::

::: {#lem-henkinization .lemma name="Consistent Henkinization" date="2026-07-12"}
For every consistent set \(\Sigma\) of \(L\)-sentences there are a language
\(L^*\supseteq L\), obtained only by adjoining constants, and a consistent
Henkin theory \(H\) of \(L^*\)-sentences with \(\Sigma\subseteq H\).
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-henkinization"}
We use two standard set-theoretic construction principles, neither of which
has logical model-existence content: the **well-ordering theorem** (every set
can be well ordered) and **transfinite recursion** (a uniquely prescribed
successor/union-limit construction along an ordinal exists).  Their
hypotheses apply to the sets of formulas generated by @def-fol-formulas.

Put \(L_0=L\), except that if \(L\) has no constant, adjoin a new constant
\(d\); put \(H_0=\Sigma\).  This remains consistent by
@lem-pure-language-extension.  Suppose \(L_n,H_n\) have been constructed and
\(H_n\) is consistent.  Well-order all existential \(L_n\)-sentences as
\((E_\alpha)_{\alpha<\kappa_n}\), writing
\(E_\alpha=\exists xA_\alpha(x)\).  By transfinite recursion, at successor
\(\alpha+1\) adjoin a new constant \(c_{n,\alpha}\) and the sentence
\[
 E_\alpha\to A_\alpha(c_{n,\alpha}).
\]
@lem-henkin-witness-consistency preserves consistency.  At a limit ordinal
take unions.  Such a union is consistent: a hypothetical contradictory
derivation is finite by @lem-proof-finitary, so the finitely many assumptions
and new constants it mentions all occur below one earlier ordinal, where the
theory is consistent.  Let \(L_{n+1}\) and \(H_{n+1}\) be the unions after
the enumeration.

Finally put \(L^*=\bigcup_{n<\omega}L_n\) and
\(H=\bigcup_{n<\omega}H_n\).  The same finite-derivation argument, now taking
the maximum of finitely many natural-number stages, proves consistency.
Every existential \(L^*\)-sentence contains finitely many symbols, hence is
already an \(L_n\)-sentence for some \(n\).  At the next stage its enumeration
adds a witness constant and its witness implication, which remains in \(H\).
Thus \(H\) has the property in @def-henkin-theory, contains \(\Sigma\), and is
consistent.
:::

::: {#thm-consistent-model .theorem name="Model existence for consistent theories" date="2026-07-12"}
For every signature \(L\) and every syntactically consistent set \(\Sigma\)
of \(L\)-sentences, there is an \(L\)-structure \(\mathcal M\) such that
\(\mathcal M\models\Sigma\).
:::

::: {.proof of="thm-consistent-model"}
Apply @lem-henkinization to obtain a consistent Henkin extension \(H\) in
\(L^*\).  Apply @lem-maximal-consistent in that fixed language to extend
\(H\) to a consistent, deductively closed, negation-complete theory \(T\).
Because every witness implication belonging to \(H\) still belongs to \(T\),
it remains Henkin by @def-henkin-theory.  Form its canonical term model
\(\mathcal M_T\) using @def-canonical-term-model.  The sentence case of
@lem-canonical-truth gives \(\mathcal M_T\models A\) for every
\(A\in T\), hence for every \(A\in\Sigma\).  Take the
\(L\)-reduct \(\mathcal M=\mathcal M_T\upharpoonright L\).  Since every
member of \(\Sigma\) is an \(L\)-sentence, @lem-reduct-satisfaction yields
\(\mathcal M\models\Sigma\).
:::
